advantages: Reachable from any shell, CI step, cron job, container, or pipeline — no editor required.
Composes with the Unix toolchain (pipes, xargs, exit-code-based gating, `&&` chaining).
A nice CLI is a small, durable API for your library — easier to ship than a GUI or HTTP service.
`--help` is documentation users actually read because it's right at the prompt.
Thin CLIs encourage clean separation between IO and business logic.
disadvantages: Easy to slip into thick CLIs that mix parsing, IO, and logic — hard to test, hard to reuse.
Sub-command growth becomes its own design problem (option naming, defaults, deprecation).
Cross-platform quirks (Windows path quoting, signal handling, console encoding) bite eventually.
Performance: Python startup adds ~80–150ms per invocation; bad for tight inner loops in scripts.
Versioning the CLI surface (flag changes, output formats) is a real maintenance burden once users depend on you.
